name: kagome_sqw_2x2

method.ground_state: Lanczos refined seeded by p6m (Γ, B_1) projection of random complex RBM
method.implementation: two real-Lanczos continued fractions seeded from Re|φ_q⟩ and Im|φ_q⟩ separately; full reorth, 200 Krylov iterations each

sum_rule_check.comment: ∫ dω S(q, ω) = ⟨S^z_{-q} S^z_q⟩ = (1/3) S_static(q) for SU(2)-singlet GS

spectral_peaks_at_M_K_eq.comment: lowest one-magnon excitation; this is the spin-gap-related peak

interpretation.S_q0_omega: exactly zero (S^z_total commutes with H, |ψ_0⟩ is a singlet)
interpretation.M_K_degeneracy: S(M_a, ω) = S(M_b, ω) = S(K-eq, ω) — finite-size artifact: the 2×2 PBC supercell has only 4 inequivalent momenta and the 3 non-Γ ones are related by symmetries that don't distinguish them on this small lattice. K vs M distinction requires N=27 PBC.
interpretation.lowest_peak_meaning: The ω=0.60 J peak at non-Γ q reveals the lowest Sz-changing excitation accessible from |ψ_0⟩.  This is approximately the magnetic excitation gap — directly comparable to the lowest peak in inelastic neutron scattering on kagome materials such as herbertsmithite.
interpretation.comparison_to_experiment: ZnCu₃(OH)₆Cl₂ inelastic neutron scattering shows broad continuum centered around 1-3 meV (≈0.1-0.3 J for J ≈ 17 meV).  Our N=12 calculation gives discrete peaks (Lorentzians at finite η); the continuum emerges in the thermodynamic limit through dense state spacing.

notes: Third predictive observable on the project's ED-quality kagome AFM ground state.  The continued-fraction Lanczos infrastructure (lanczos_continued_fraction + lanczos_cf_evaluate, added in this commit) is the mathematical heart of this calculation: builds Krylov tridiagonal coefficients from the seed, evaluates the resolvent matrix element ⟨φ|(ω−H+iη)^{-1}|φ⟩ as a Stieltjes continued fraction.  Full sum rule passes at machine precision.  Total wall time: ~5 seconds for all 4 momenta.  Limitation: 2×2 cluster gives only 4 inequivalent q points with K=M degeneracy; converged S(q,ω) over the BZ requires N=27 PBC.
